steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the main idea of the passage. The passage establishes that the Countess has only trivial flaws — ones the Milby ladies share — yet the ladies conclude she must have serious hidden vices to justify the distinction they feel separates them from her. The logic moves backward: disapproval is decided first, then a reason is invented.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘The residents of Milby had initially viewed the Countess Czerlaski with suspicion, but they have gradually come to see her as mostly harmless.’: The passage shows no softening of attitude — the ladies are actively constructing reasons to disapprove, not relaxing their judgment. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Although the Countess Czerlaski actively cultivates a sense of mystery about her, her background is less interesting than what many residents of Milby have been led to believe.’: The passage never suggests the Countess is managing her own image. The focus is entirely on the ladies’ reasoning, not her actions. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Although the residents of Milby are generally thought to be hospitable, the Countess Czerlaski has committed many social blunders that have caused her to be widely disliked.’: The passage explicitly says her flaws are slight and shared by the ladies themselves — not that she has committed blunders that justify dislike. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Some residents of Milby are determined to disapprove of the Countess Czerlaski even though the reasons for their dislike are largely groundless.’: Matches the passage exactly. The ladies know her faults are trivial and shared, yet they reason backward to invent graver vices to justify a distinction they’re already committed to making. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
